ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies existing tools (Probabilistic Soft Logic compiled into hinge-loss MRFs) to the task of political frame detection on Twitter and combines established lexical features with social/behavioral predicates (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS). While adapting Boydstun et al.’s codebook to create Twitter-specific frames and assembling a congressional tweet corpus are useful contributions, they represent an application/adaptation rather than a fundamentally new method. Overall this is an incremental but practically meaningful extension rather than a highly novel methodological advance. | B(3): The paper presents a moderate novelty by applying weakly supervised Probabilistic Soft Logic (compiled into hinge-loss MRFs) and by combining lexical features with network/behavioral predicates (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS), yielding a large empirical gain (+21.52 unsupervised F1) for political frame detection on Twitter. However, the methodological core (PSL-based collective classification and use of social/network signals) are existing techniques applied to a new dataset and adapted task (Twitter-specific frames and a congressional tweets corpus), so the contribution is an incremental but useful extension rather than a fundamentally new idea.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies existing tools (Probabilistic Soft Logic compiled into hinge-loss MRFs) to the task of political frame detection on Twitter and combines established lexical features with social/behavioral predicates (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS). While adapting Boydstun et al.’s codebook to create Twitter-specific frames and assembling a congressional tweet corpus are useful contributions, they represent an application/adaptation rather than a fundamentally new method. Overall this is an incremental but practically meaningful extension rather than a highly novel methodological advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a moderate novelty by applying weakly supervised Probabilistic Soft Logic (compiled into hinge-loss MRFs) and by combining lexical features with network/behavioral predicates (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS), yielding a large empirical gain (+21.52 unsupervised F1) for political frame detection on Twitter. However, the methodological core (PSL-based collective classification and use of social/network signals) are existing techniques applied to a new dataset and adapted task (Twitter-specific frames and a congressional tweets corpus), so the contribution is an incremental but useful extension rather than a fundamentally new idea.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles an NLP task (frame classification of political tweets) using probabilistic graphical models (PSL / hinge-loss MRF) and collective classification techniques, and it presents a large collected corpus (184,914 tweets) plus manual annotations. The audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in weak supervision, structured prediction, and social-media language) is appropriate, and the problem formulation and evaluation (average unsupervised F1; inter-annotator Cohen’s Kappa reported) match expectations for the venue. Minor concerns for a top-tier conference are limited reproducibility details (hyperparameters not reported, code availability not mentioned) and the relatively small manually annotated subset (40 politicians), which are scope/reproducibility issues rather than misalignment with the venue. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within NLP/ML venue interests: it tackles political framing in Twitter (an NLP task) and develops weakly supervised collective classification models using Probabilistic Soft Logic compiled into hinge-loss MRFs, which will interest audiences working on weak supervision and structured prediction. The use of social/behavioral predicates (RETWEETS, FOLLOWS, SAMETIME) combined with lexical signals and a sizeable congressional tweet corpus (184,914 tweets) plus planned data/rule release fit conference norms. My only reservation is that the contribution appears application-focused and relies on existing PSL machinery rather than introducing substantial new ML methodology, which may limit appeal at the most theory/method-driven ML venues.

The audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in weak supervision, structured prediction, and social-media language) is appropriate, and the problem formulation and evaluation (average unsupervised F1; inter-annotator Cohen’s Kappa reported) match expectations for the venue. Minor concerns for a top-tier conference are limited reproducibility details (hyperparameters not reported, code availability not mentioned) and the relatively small manually annotated subset (40 politicians), which are scope/reproducibility issues rather than misalignment with the venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within NLP/ML venue interests: it tackles political framing in Twitter (an NLP task) and develops weakly supervised collective classification models using Probabilistic Soft Logic compiled into hinge-loss MRFs, which will interest audiences working on weak supervision and structured prediction. The use of social/behavioral predicates (RETWEETS, FOLLOWS, SAMETIME) combined with lexical signals and a sizeable congressional tweet corpus (184,914 tweets) plus planned data/rule release fit conference norms. My only reservation is that the contribution appears application-focused and relies on existing PSL machinery rather than introducing substantial new ML methodology, which may limit appeal at the most theory/method-driven ML venues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper compares its PSL models to a lexical baseline and reports a substantial 21.52-point average unsupervised F1 gain on the authors' congressional tweet corpus using the same metric and data, suggesting fair in‑dataset comparisons. However, only a single (lexical) baseline is reported, so important competing approaches (e.g., supervised classifiers, prior network- or PSL-based methods) appear missing despite the summary noting thorough related-work coverage. The paper does report limitations (e.g., inter-annotator agreement) and quantifies improvements, but comparisons are incomplete relative to the state of the art. | B(3): The paper reports results only against a lexical baseline (claiming a 21.52-point average unsupervised F1 gain) on their collected congressional tweets using the same evaluation metric, so the direct comparison appears internally fair. However, it omits other important baselines (e.g., supervised classifiers or other weakly supervised/PSL approaches) and does not report or acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better. Although related work coverage is described as thorough, the empirical comparisons are limited, making the evaluation incomplete.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its PSL models to a lexical baseline and reports a substantial 21.52-point average unsupervised F1 gain on the authors' congressional tweet corpus using the same metric and data, suggesting fair in‑dataset comparisons. However, only a single (lexical) baseline is reported, so important competing approaches (e.g., supervised classifiers, prior network- or PSL-based methods) appear missing despite the summary noting thorough related-work coverage. The paper does report limitations (e.g., inter-annotator agreement) and quantifies improvements, but comparisons are incomplete relative to the state of the art.

Although related work coverage is described as thorough, the empirical comparisons are limited, making the evaluation incomplete.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well organized with a logical flow (intro -> methods using PSL -> experiments on 184,914 congressional tweets -> conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (weakly supervised PSL models leveraging lexical and social/behavioral features, dataset and rules to be released). Writing quality is reported as good, related work coverage thorough, and figures/tables (e.g., Table 2 statistics) are effective and informative. A minor presentation shortcoming is that some methodological/detail-level information (notation and hyperparameter reporting) is not clearly documented in the summary, which slightly reduces reproducibility and clarity of the methods section. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from methods (PSL models compiled into hinge-loss MRFs) to experiments (evaluation with average unsupervised F1 and Cohen’s Kappa) and conclusions, and the main contributions (weakly supervised PSL leveraging lexical and behavioral features, new Twitter-specific frames, and a large Congress tweet corpus) are stated early. Notation and presentation seem coherent enough to follow the modeling approach, and figures/tables are used (e.g., Table 2 for dataset statistics) to support the narrative. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, but there are minor presentation omissions that affect clarity—hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned—so a small amount of additional detail would improve reproducibility and clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well organized with a logical flow (intro -> methods using PSL -> experiments on 184,914 congressional tweets -> conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (weakly supervised PSL models leveraging lexical and social/behavioral features, dataset and rules to be released). IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important task of identifying policy frames in political tweets using weakly supervised Probabilistic Soft Logic models that combine lexical features with social/behavioral signals (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS). The reported 21.52-point average unsupervised F1 gain over a lexical baseline, plus a substantial corpus of 184,914 Congressional tweets and planned release of data and PSL rules, indicate results that could shift researchers toward incorporating behavioral/network information and collective inference for framing detection. By adapting Boydstun et al.’s codebook to Twitter (including new Twitter-specific frames), it opens clear new directions for weak supervision and computational political discourse analysis, giving it strong impact though not necessarily transformative across all of NLP. | B(4): The paper tackles an important problem—automated framing detection in political Twitter discourse—and presents a concrete weakly supervised approach (PSL models) that leverages both lexical and social/behavioral signals. The reported large gain (21.52-point average unsupervised F1 improvement over a lexical baseline), the compilation of a sizable congressional tweet corpus (184,914 tweets) with annotations from 40 politicians, and the release of PSL rule sets and adapted Twitter-specific frames suggest the work could materially change practice and be reused by researchers. While not necessarily transformative for all of NLP, it strongly advances collective classification and computational political communication and opens clear avenues for follow-up work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important task of identifying policy frames in political tweets using weakly supervised Probabilistic Soft Logic models that combine lexical features with social/behavioral signals (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS). The reported 21.52-point average unsupervised F1 gain over a lexical baseline, plus a substantial corpus of 184,914 Congressional tweets and planned release of data and PSL rules, indicate results that could shift researchers toward incorporating behavioral/network information and collective inference for framing detection. By adapting Boydstun et al.’s codebook to Twitter (including new Twitter-specific frames), it opens clear new directions for weak supervision and computational political discourse analysis, giving it strong impact though not necessarily transformative across all of NLP.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—automated framing detection in political Twitter discourse—and presents a concrete weakly supervised approach (PSL models) that leverages both lexical and social/behavioral signals. The reported large gain (21.52-point average unsupervised F1 improvement over a lexical baseline), the compilation of a sizable congressional tweet corpus (184,914 tweets) with annotations from 40 politicians, and the release of PSL rule sets and adapted Twitter-specific frames suggest the work could materially change practice and be reused by researchers. While not necessarily transformative for all of NLP, it strongly advances collective classification and computational political communication and opens clear avenues for follow-up work.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments demonstrate a substantial gain (21.52-point average unsupervised F1) on a large collected corpus with 184,914 tweets and annotated data from 40 politicians, but comparisons appear limited mainly to a lexical baseline and hyperparameters are not reported. Ablation or sensitivity analyses are not clearly described beyond the lexical vs combined-feature comparison. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the paper acknowledges several limitations (73.4% IAA, multi-frame tweets, adapting/dropping frames), however the absence of hyperparameter details and unclear code availability reduce reproducibility. | B(3): The paper evaluates a large corpus (184,914 tweets) and shows a substantial +21.52 point gain over a lexical baseline while introducing Twitter-specific frames and reporting inter-annotator agreement, so the experiments are reasonably substantial. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported (e.g., contribution of individual behavioral predicates), and only a single baseline (lexical) is mentioned. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations (annotation difficulty, multi-frame tweets, dropping/adding frames) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is mixed: the authors promise to release the dataset and PSL rules but do not report hyperparameters or mention code availability, limiting full replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments demonstrate a substantial gain (21.52-point average unsupervised F1) on a large collected corpus with 184,914 tweets and annotated data from 40 politicians, but comparisons appear limited mainly to a lexical baseline and hyperparameters are not reported. Ablation or sensitivity analyses are not clearly described beyond the lexical vs combined-feature comparison. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the paper acknowledges several limitations (73.4% IAA, multi-frame tweets, adapting/dropping frames), however the absence of hyperparameter details and unclear code availability reduce reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates a large corpus (184,914 tweets) and shows a substantial +21.52 point gain over a lexical baseline while introducing Twitter-specific frames and reporting inter-annotator agreement, so the experiments are reasonably substantial. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported (e.g., contribution of individual behavioral predicates), and only a single baseline (lexical) is mentioned. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations (annotation difficulty, multi-frame tweets, dropping/adding frames) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is mixed: the authors promise to release the dataset and PSL rules but do not report hyperparameters or mention code availability, limiting full replication.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper’s main claim—that weakly supervised PSL models leveraging lexical plus network/behavioral features improve frame prediction—is supported by experimental results (reported 21.52-point average unsupervised F1 gain over a lexical baseline) and a sizeable unlabeled corpus (184,914 tweets). However, there are methodological concerns that reduce confidence: evaluation relies on a single lexical baseline, hyperparameters and statistical significance testing are not reported, and the labeled evaluation set is relatively small (manual annotations from 40 politicians) with only moderate inter-annotator agreement (73.4%). Given these gaps, the work appears technically plausible but some key experimental controls and reporting are missing, so the technical assessment is cautiously positive. | B(3): The paper states clear technical claims (PSL-based collective classification combining lexical and network features, compiled to a hinge-loss MRF) and reports a substantial empirical gain (21.52 points average unsupervised F1 over a lexical baseline). However, key experimental details are missing or weakly supported in the summary: hyperparameters are not reported, statistical tests are not mentioned, and the annotated evaluation set is small (tweets from 40 politicians) with only moderate inter-annotator agreement (73.4%), which limits confidence in the robustness and generality of the reported improvement. Mathematically the approach is plausible and there are no obvious derivation errors in the summary, but the evidence provided is insufficiently thorough to fully validate all empirical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claim—that weakly supervised PSL models leveraging lexical plus network/behavioral features improve frame prediction—is supported by experimental results (reported 21.52-point average unsupervised F1 gain over a lexical baseline) and a sizeable unlabeled corpus (184,914 tweets). However, there are methodological concerns that reduce confidence: evaluation relies on a single lexical baseline, hyperparameters and statistical significance testing are not reported, and the labeled evaluation set is relatively small (manual annotations from 40 politicians) with only moderate inter-annotator agreement (73.4%). Given these gaps, the work appears technically plausible but some key experimental controls and reporting are missing, so the technical assessment is cautiously positive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (PSL-based collective classification combining lexical and network features, compiled to a hinge-loss MRF) and reports a substantial empirical gain (21.52 points average unsupervised F1 over a lexical baseline). However, key experimental details are missing or weakly supported in the summary: hyperparameters are not reported, statistical tests are not mentioned, and the annotated evaluation set is small (tweets from 40 politicians) with only moderate inter-annotator agreement (73.4%), which limits confidence in the robustness and generality of the reported improvement. Mathematically the approach is plausible and there are no obvious derivation errors in the summary, but the evidence provided is insufficiently thorough to fully validate all empirical claims.

main_claims: They present weakly supervised Probabilistic Soft Logic (PSL) collective classification models that leverage both lexical features and social/behavioral information to predict policy frames in political tweets.
Combining lexical features with network-based behavioral features (temporal activity, retweets, follows) increases the average unsupervised F1 score by 21.52 points over a lexical baseline alone.
They compiled a corpus of 184,914 tweets from members of the U.S. Congress and produced manually annotated tweets from 40 politicians; the dataset and full PSL rule lists will be released.
Their PSL rules are compiled into a hinge-loss Markov random field to perform frame prediction, and they introduce Twitter-specific frames to adapt Boydstun et al.’s codebook for microblog data.
_summary.methodology: They build weakly supervised PSL models (compiled into hinge-loss MRFs) that use language-based predicates (unigrams, MAXSIM word2vec similarity, party bigrams/trigrams) and behavior-based predicates (SAMETIME, RETWEETS, FOLLOWS) for collective classification of frames in tweets.
datasets: 184,914 tweets of members of the U.S. Congress (collected by the authors)
Annotated tweets from 40 politicians (10 Republicans and 10 Democrats from both House and Senate); statistics presented in Table 2; dataset to be released
limitations: Inter-annotator agreement is 73.4%, indicating the difficulty of frame classification for tweets.
Tweets are often compound and may contain multiple frames, requiring special handling during annotation and modeling.
Boydstun’s original Frame 15 (Other) was never used for tweets, necessitating dropping it and proposing three Twitter-specific frames.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - political framing / collective classification on Twitter
